modules.module: M1: Why Most Side Projects Die and How Agile Fixes That

Claude.notes: Strong opener: Napster vs. major-label labs frames the speed-of-learning thesis cleanly, then four named protagonists (Marcus, Priya, Jordan, Amara) each carry one lesson's concept. Story-creates-problem is consistently tight — Marcus's deferred-decision stall is exactly what Agile sprint cadence answers. The narrator does occasionally hand the insight to the reader rather than letting the protagonist arrive at it.
Claude.topIssue: Lessons frequently state the takeaway in narrator voice ('What killed the project wasn't lack of skill...') instead of letting Marcus or Priya arrive at the realization on the page.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of Agile, Waterfall, MVP, and velocity are correct at the right depth, and the LIRA framework is presented as the course's invention (which is fair). However, the Napster opener has a factual error — Shawn Fanning was 18 when he released Napster in June 1999, not 22 — and the framing of 'major labels spent those same months in meeting rooms' compresses a more complex industry response into a tidy contrast. Lab outcome quality also depends on the AI-partner chat actually pushing back, which the lesson promises but the static text can't guarantee.
Gemini.topIssue: Factual error in the intro: Shawn Fanning was 18 when Napster shipped in 1999, not 22 (he was a Northeastern freshman, not graduating-age).

Perplexity.notes: Currency is excellent. The lesson explicitly grounds itself in 2024–2025, names the right tools (Cursor, Replit, Lovable, Claude), and the protagonist scenarios feel like they could happen this semester. Napster is a 1999 reference but it's recognizable enough to undergrads to land. The lab framework (sprint autopsy with AI partner) maps cleanly onto how 2025 builders actually work.
Perplexity.topIssue: Napster as the opener is from 1999 — it works, but a parallel 2024 example (e.g., a Lovable user shipping a niche tool in days) would feel more native to today's learner.

Claude.notes: Marcus's vague-app monologue is one of the best opening beats in the course — it perfectly creates the 'specificity is a missing skill' problem the lesson then solves. The Customer Specificity Ladder, Five Whys, and First Transaction frameworks are well-sequenced. But 'Marcus' is a different person here than in M1 (UC San Diego cog-sci vs. Georgia Tech CS/econ), and Priya, Jasmine, and Theo also reappear across modules with different backstories — character names are being recycled rather than built.
Claude.topIssue: Protagonist name reuse with different identities across modules (Marcus M1 ≠ Marcus M2; Priya M1 ≠ Priya M2/M3/M5/M7) breaks character consistency.

Gemini.notes: MVP and Five Whys are defined correctly. The 'first transaction' framing is treated as the operational core of an MVP, which is a defensible position but worth flagging — Eric Ries's canonical MVP is 'minimum experiment to test a hypothesis,' not necessarily 'first dollar.' The lesson should attribute Five Whys to Sakichi Toyoda / Toyota Production System rather than presenting it as a generic product technique.
Gemini.topIssue: Five Whys is presented without attribution; in a course aimed at builders, a one-line 'developed by Sakichi Toyoda at Toyota' would prevent students from re-encountering it later as 'new.'

Perplexity.notes: Scenarios feel current — NYU entrepreneurship pitches, Notion docs, $40/month indie templates. The 'roommate's $40/month Notion template business with three paying clients' is exactly the kind of micro-SaaS comparison that resonates in 2025. The Customer Specificity Ladder reads as something a learner could apply tomorrow on Reddit/Discord.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module skips the orientation/intro tab that M1 had — a learner arriving at M2 directly is dropped into Lesson 1 with no 'where you are in the course' context.

modules.module: M3: Why Solo Builders Need Sprints More Than Teams Do

Claude.notes: Marcus's eleven-half-finished-features stall is the strongest 'story creates the problem' beat in the course — the infinite-horizon trap concept lands hard because Marcus's pain is so specific. Vertical slicing, dependency inflation, retrospective framework, and the three-horizon roadmap are all introduced at the right altitude for an Advanced learner. Marcus name re-use continues to nag.
Claude.topIssue: Marcus's '11 half-finished features' setup is great, but the lesson doesn't show him discovering that this is a sprint-shape problem — the narrator names the trap for him.

Gemini.notes: Sprint, retrospective, and roadmap definitions are accurate. Vertical slicing is correctly distinguished from horizontal slicing in the lab framing. 'Implementation intentions' is referenced as habit-formation research without citation — Peter Gollwitzer's work would be the right anchor. Decisional energy / cognitive drift framing is essentially Roy Baumeister-adjacent decision-fatigue territory and would benefit from a one-line attribution.
Gemini.topIssue: 'Implementation intentions' is invoked as established research without naming Peter Gollwitzer (1999) — fine pedagogically, but a learner googling the term should find the source.

Perplexity.notes: Sprint-Zero and 'pivot vs. distraction' framing line up with how 2024–2025 indie builders actually talk about their work. The lab roles (Sprint Coach, Scope Surgeon, Retro Facilitator, Devil's Advocate Strategist) are all current product-coaching archetypes. No dated examples flagged.
Perplexity.topIssue: No materially dated content; the only freshening would be naming a current 2025 indie-hacker exemplar in the discussion of solo sprint discipline.

modules.module: M4: AI Tools for Every Stage — Research, Build, Test, Iterate

Claude.notes: Strongest module on cognitive framing — the 'why each AI stage requires a different question' thesis is exactly the kind of meta-question Advanced learners should be asked to hold. Marcus, Priya, Devon, and Aisha each anchor a different stage cleanly. 'AI amplifies in whichever direction you're already pointed' is a quotable line and an accurate one.
Claude.topIssue: The four stages (research/build/test/iterate) get one lesson each, which is dense — by Lesson 4 the learner is being asked to internalize four distinct AI postures in a single module.

Gemini.notes: Tool descriptions are accurate: Copilot/Cursor/Claude Artifacts as code-gen accelerators, Mixpanel as event analytics, the inherent tradeoff that scope-cheap tools amplify scope creep. The lesson treats 'AI as your harshest critic' as a posture — true, but a sharper version would name techniques (red-teaming prompts, adversarial review prompts) the learner can paste.
Gemini.topIssue: 'AI as harshest critic' is asserted without giving the learner a concrete adversarial-review prompt template they could copy and use in Cursor/Claude.

Perplexity.notes: Strongest currency in the course. Cursor, Copilot, Claude Artifacts, Mixpanel, Discord, Reddit r/freelance — all named, all the right shape for 2025 solo builders. NIL deals as a Priya example is current and specific. The 'launched on a Tuesday, broken by Thursday' bug pattern with 5/7 churn is exactly what a 2025 ProductHunt launch looks like.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module is current; the only freshening would be naming a 2025-specific testing tool (e.g., Playwright codegen with AI assist) alongside the generic 'AI can help generate test cases.'

modules.module: M5: Handling Scope Creep When AI Makes Everything Feel Possible

Claude.notes: Best conceptual contribution in the course. The 'Yes Machine Problem' — that LLMs are structurally biased toward additive responses because 'helpful' in brainstorming context means more — is a sharp, original frame for AI scope creep, and Maya's six-week-becomes-two-year-startup story makes it concrete. The Minimum Viable Boundary and triage session are usable artifacts.
Claude.topIssue: Maya's collapse happens off-page ('Three hours later she had a document...') — showing one or two of the actual 'what if it also did X?' exchanges would let the learner see the trap operating in real time.

Gemini.notes: The 'structurally biased toward expansion' claim about LLMs is technically accurate but worth precisifying — RLHF training on human preference data does favor longer, more comprehensive responses on brainstorming tasks, and 'sycophancy' has been measured in published evaluations. Naming RLHF or instruction-tuning would give the claim a load-bearing source.
Gemini.topIssue: 'LLMs are structurally biased toward expansion' is correct but uncited — RLHF and sycophancy literature (e.g., Anthropic's sycophancy work) would be a one-line attribution.

Perplexity.notes: Yes Machine framing names a real, current pain — anyone using Cursor or Claude in 2025 has felt this. The MVB constraint card ('Donation page. One nonprofit. Working by September 1') is the kind of artifact a learner could literally tape to their monitor today.
Perplexity.topIssue: Examples could name current real-world cases of AI-assisted scope creep (e.g., a public Lovable/Cursor postmortem) to anchor the framework in observed 2025 builder behavior.

Claude.notes: Highest-scoring module. Marcus's 'roommates loved it, 11 of 340 came back' opener is a textbook story-creates-problem beat, and the preference-vs-workflow feedback distinction is the kind of concept Advanced learners can carry into any product context. Camille's pantry-app pivot is the cleanest example of pivot-vs-iterate in the course.
Claude.topIssue: The Dev → 'three users said the same thing in different words' moment is the most teachable beat in the course — it deserves a callout box, not just an inline mention.

Gemini.notes: Rob Fitzpatrick is correctly named as the source of 'compliment-shaped noise' (he's the author of 'The Mom Test', 2013) — best-attributed concept in the course. Politeness loop, opinion-vs-behavior, and pivot-vs-iterate definitions are all defensible. 'Would you use this?' as a question that produces near-universal yeses is consistent with established user-research literature.
Gemini.topIssue: Fitzpatrick is named but his book ('The Mom Test') isn't — a learner who liked this lesson should be told what to read next.

Perplexity.notes: Cold outreach via Reddit r/freelance, r/graphic_design, r/freelancers reads as the actual playbook 2025 indie builders use. TikTok-launched 600 signups → 4% retention is also a current shape. Discord-power-user bias is a 2024–2025 observation that lands.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 'three platforms, 11 of 40 responded' Reddit cold-outreach numbers feel realistic for 2024 but Reddit's current API/search environment in 2025 is more restrictive — a learner trying to replicate may need updated tactics.

modules.module: M7: Turning a Side Project into a Portfolio Piece or Income Stream

Claude.notes: Priya's 'It sounds like the AI did most of the work' interview moment is the most resonant scene in the course — and the most career-relevant for the actual target learner. The hook formula '[User] had [problem]. I built [tool] to [outcome]. It now [evidence]' is a portable, defensible artifact.
Claude.topIssue: The Yusuf 1,400-user shutdown story is good, but he 'shuts the tool down because he didn't know what else to do' — the lesson should let Yusuf make a decision (rate-limit + email users) instead of having the narrator state what was missing.

Gemini.notes: Pricing logic (value-based vs. cost-based, free-tier psychology) is consistent with established SaaS pricing literature. The 'responsibility curve' is a useful mental model but presented as a course coinage — a brief acknowledgment that this overlaps with established product-ethics frameworks (e.g., Karen Hao / RAI literature) would strengthen it.
Gemini.topIssue: $340 OpenAI bill from 1,400 users in 4 days is plausible but the lesson doesn't show the math — a learner can't sanity-check whether their own tool would burn similarly.

Perplexity.notes: Substack writer at 200 subscribers / $7/month / $800/month is realistic 2024–2025 indie publishing economics. OpenAI cost exposure for student tools is a current and recurring failure mode. The 'AI did most of the work' interview line is exactly the 2025 hiring conversation.
Perplexity.topIssue: Substack/$6/month newsletter pricing example is current but the AI side of pricing (tokens-per-user economics for an LLM-backed tool) is sketched lightly — current builders need rougher unit-economics math.

modules.module: M8: Your First Sprint — Choosing What to Build in 30 Days

Claude.notes: Capstone module. The 4-week sprint structure (Foundation / First User / Hardening + Second User / Launch) is a clean synthesis of M1–M7 frameworks. The 85% rule and 'launch avoidance loop' are genuinely useful concepts. Some thematic overlap with M1 ('picking is the hard part') means the narrative impact is slightly diminished by the time the learner arrives here.
Claude.topIssue: Capstone re-treads M1's 'picking is the hard part' framing without adding a new conceptual layer — Maya's option-paralysis story echoes Marcus's deferred-decision story.

Gemini.notes: 85% rule is a defensible heuristic; 'option paralysis' / Barry Schwartz's paradox-of-choice is invoked correctly without being named. Three-types-of-stuck taxonomy (Technical / Domain / Motivation) is a clean diagnostic framework. Sprint structure (foundation → real user → harden → launch) is consistent with established lean/agile practice.
Gemini.topIssue: 'Option paralysis' is named without crediting Barry Schwartz's paradox-of-choice work — a one-line citation would help learners follow up.

Perplexity.notes: 30-day sprint format aligns with current indie-hacker / build-in-public norms (#buildinpublic on X, Lovable launch threads, Replit ship-it-Friday culture). Recovery protocol concept is a 2024–2025 lean-startup adaptation that learners can use this week.
Perplexity.topIssue: References to 'TikTok' and 'Twitter' for launch — accurate but Twitter is now X and the 2025 launch surface (X / Reddit / Lovable Discover / ProductHunt) is more fragmented than the lesson implies.
